query:
You are working on the pinterest/ktlint repository.
The codebase is at version 0.49 (commit: cbee8223).

## Issue to Resolve

Repeat format step until no more errors are found which can be auto-corrected
The order in which rules are executed can be influences with `Rule.VisitorModifier`. Now the network or rule execution dependencies get more complex, it becomes difficult to find that single order of rules which always resolves all format errors in a single trip. This is confusing for users of kltint.

If format finds any lint violations in a file that can be autocorrected, it should automatically rerun format on that file before returning the lint violations of the last format run. To prevent that a set of rules cause an infinite rerun of the format, no more than  3 reruns will be executed before returning the results.

## Relevant Files

The following files are likely relevant to solving this issue:
- `CHANGELOG.md`
- `RELEASE_TESTING.MD`
- `documentation/snapshot/docs/rules/experimental.md`
- `ktlint-rule-engine-core/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/rule/engine/core/api/ASTNodeExtension.kt`
- `ktlint-rule-engine/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/rule/engine/api/Code.kt`
- `ktlint-rule-engine/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/rule/engine/api/KtLintRuleEngine.kt`
- `ktlint-ruleset-standard/build.gradle.kts`
- `ktlint-ruleset-standard/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/ruleset/standard/rules/ArgumentListWrappingRule.kt`
- `ktlint-ruleset-standard/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/ruleset/standard/rules/BinaryExpressionWrappingRule.kt`
- `ktlint-ruleset-standard/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/ruleset/standard/rules/BlankLineBeforeDeclarationRule.kt`
- `ktlint-ruleset-standard/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/ruleset/standard/rules/IfElseWrappingRule.kt`
- `ktlint-ruleset-standard/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/ruleset/standard/rules/ParameterListWrappingRule.kt`
- `ktlint-ruleset-standard/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/ruleset/standard/rules/StringTemplateIndentRule.kt`
- `ktlint-ruleset-standard/src/main/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/ruleset/standard/rules/WrappingRule.kt`

## Instructions

Please analyze this issue and implement a fix. Make the necessary code changes to resolve the problem described above.

Focus on:
- Understanding the root cause of the issue
- Making minimal, targeted changes to fix the problem
- Ensuring your changes don't break existing functionality

## Important Evaluation Notes

**Your work will be evaluated by running hidden tests that you do not have access to.** These tests will verify that your fix correctly addresses the issue. You should be confident in your solution before finishing.
**If you write tests to verify your changes, create them in NEW test files only** - do NOT modify existing test files

## CRITICAL RESTRICTIONS

**NEVER use git commands.** Do not run `git status`, `git diff`, `git checkout`, `git reset`, or ANY other git commands. Using git will corrupt the evaluation environment and invalidate your entire submission. All file operations should be done through normal file editing and reading tools, not git.

1→@file:Suppress("MemberVisibilityCanBePrivate")
  2→
  3→package com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api
  4→
  5→import com.pinterest.ktlint.logger.api.initKtLintKLogger
  6→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.EditorConfigDefaults.Companion.EMPTY_EDITOR_CONFIG_DEFAULTS
  7→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.EditorConfigOverride.Companion.EMPTY_EDITOR_CONFIG_OVERRIDE
  8→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.Rule
  9→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.RuleProvider
 10→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.editorconfig.CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY
 11→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.editorconfig.CodeStyleValue
 12→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.editorconfig.END_OF_LINE_PROPERTY
 13→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.api.propertyTypes
 14→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.core.util.safeAs
 15→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.internal.EditorConfigFinder
 16→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.internal.EditorConfigGenerator
 17→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.internal.EditorConfigLoader
 18→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.internal.EditorConfigLoaderEc4j
 19→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.internal.RuleExecutionContext
 20→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.internal.RuleExecutionContext.Companion.createRuleExecutionContext
 21→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.internal.ThreadSafeEditorConfigCache.Companion.THREAD_SAFE_EDITOR_CONFIG_CACHE
 22→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.internal.VisitorProvider
 23→import mu.KotlinLogging
 24→import org.ec4j.core.Resource
 25→import org.ec4j.core.model.PropertyType.EndOfLineValue.crlf
 26→import org.ec4j.core.model.PropertyType.EndOfLineValue.lf
 27→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.lang.FileASTNode
 28→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.utils.addToStdlib.ifFalse
 29→import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets
 30→import java.nio.file.FileSystem
 31→import java.nio.file.FileSystems
 32→import java.nio.file.Path
 33→
 34→private val LOGGER = KotlinLogging.logger {}.initKtLintKLogger()
 35→
 36→public class KtLintRuleEngine(
 37→    /**
 38→     * The set of [RuleProvider]s to be invoked by the [KtLintRuleEngine]. A [RuleProvider] is able to create a new instance of a [Rule] so
 39→     * that it can keep internal state and be called thread-safe manner
 40→     */
 41→    public val ruleProviders: Set<RuleProvider> = emptySet(),
 42→    /**
 43→     * The default values for `.editorconfig` properties which are not set explicitly in any '.editorconfig' file located on the path of the
 44→     * file which is processed with the [KtLintRuleEngine]. If a property is set in [editorConfigDefaults] this takes precedence above the
 45→     * default values defined in the KtLint project.
 46→     */
 47→    public val editorConfigDefaults: EditorConfigDefaults = EMPTY_EDITOR_CONFIG_DEFAULTS,
 48→    /**
 49→     * Override values for `.editorconfig` properties. If a property is set in [editorConfigOverride] it takes precedence above the same
 50→     * property being set in any other way.
 51→     */
 52→    public val editorConfigOverride: EditorConfigOverride = EMPTY_EDITOR_CONFIG_OVERRIDE,
 53→    /**
 54→     * Temporary flag to indicate that kotlin embeddable compiler is to be executed with (default) or without the extension point
 55→     * 'org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler'. This extension point is not (yet) supported in the preview of Kotlin 1.9. Some
 56→     * rules might no longer work and throw exceptions at runtime.
 57→     * It is unclear whether the extension point will be supported. Disabling this flag on the Kotlin 1.8 compiler has the same effect. As
 58→     * of that it can be used to assess the impact, and to fix rules before release of the Ktlint version which will be based on Kotlin 1.9.
 59→     */
 60→    public val enableKotlinCompilerExtensionPoint: Boolean = true,
 61→    /**
 62→     * **For internal use only**: indicates that linting was invoked from KtLint CLI tool. It enables some internals workarounds for Kotlin
 63→     * Compiler initialization. This property is likely to be removed in any of next versions without further notice.
 64→     */
 65→    public val isInvokedFromCli: Boolean = false,
 66→    /**
 67→     * The [FileSystem] to be used. This property is primarily intended to be used in unit tests. By specifying an alternative [FileSystem]
 68→     * the unit test gains control on whether the [EditorConfigLoader] should or should not read specific ".editorconfig" files. For
 69→     * example, it is considered unwanted that a unit test is influenced by the ".editorconfig" file of the project in which the unit test
 70→     * is included.
 71→     */
 72→    public val fileSystem: FileSystem = FileSystems.getDefault(),
 73→) {
 74→    init {
 75→        require(ruleProviders.any()) {
 76→            "A non-empty set of 'ruleProviders' need to be provided"
 77→        }
 78→    }
 79→
 80→    internal val editorConfigLoaderEc4j = EditorConfigLoaderEc4j(ruleProviders.propertyTypes())
 81→
 82→    internal val editorConfigLoader =
 83→        EditorConfigLoader(
 84→            fileSystem,
 85→            editorConfigLoaderEc4j,
 86→            editorConfigDefaults,
 87→            editorConfigOverride,
 88→        )
 89→
 90→    /**
 91→     * Check the [code] for lint errors. If [code] is path as file reference then the '.editorconfig' files on the path to file are taken
 92→     * into account. For each lint violation found, the [callback] is invoked.
 93→     *
 94→     * @throws KtLintParseException if text is not a valid Kotlin code
 95→     * @throws KtLintRuleException in case of internal failure caused by a bug in rule implementation
 96→     */
 97→    public fun lint(
 98→        code: Code,
 99→        callback: (LintError) -> Unit = { },
100→    ) {
101→        LOGGER.debug { "Starting with linting file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}'" }
102→
103→        val ruleExecutionContext = createRuleExecutionContext(this, code)
104→        val errors = mutableListOf<LintError>()
105→
106→        VisitorProvider(ruleExecutionContext.ruleProviders)
107→            .visitor()
108→            .invoke { rule ->
109→                ruleExecutionContext.executeRule(rule, false) { offset, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected ->
110→                    val (line, col) = ruleExecutionContext.positionInTextLocator(offset)
111→                    errors.add(LintError(line, col, rule.ruleId, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected))
112→                }
113→            }
114→
115→        errors
116→            .sortedWith { l, r -> if (l.line != r.line) l.line - r.line else l.col - r.col }
117→            .forEach { e -> callback(e) }
118→
119→        LOGGER.debug { "Finished with linting file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}'" }
120→    }
121→
122→    /**
123→     * Fix style violations in [code] for lint errors when possible. If [code] is passed as file reference then the '.editorconfig' files on
124→     * the path are taken into account. For each lint violation found, the [callback] is invoked.
125→     *
126→     * @throws KtLintParseException if text is not a valid Kotlin code
127→     * @throws KtLintRuleException in case of internal failure caused by a bug in rule implementation
128→     */
129→    public fun format(
130→        code: Code,
131→        callback: (LintError, Boolean) -> Unit = { _, _ -> },
132→    ): String {
133→        LOGGER.debug { "Starting with formatting file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}'" }
134→
135→        val hasUTF8BOM = code.content.startsWith(UTF8_BOM)
136→        val ruleExecutionContext = createRuleExecutionContext(this, code)
137→
138→        var tripped = false
139→        var mutated = false
140→        val errors = mutableSetOf<Pair<LintError, Boolean>>()
141→        val visitorProvider = VisitorProvider(ruleExecutionContext.ruleProviders)
142→        visitorProvider
143→            .visitor()
144→            .invoke { rule ->
145→                ruleExecutionContext.executeRule(rule, true) { offset, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected ->
146→                    tripped = true
147→                    if (canBeAutoCorrected) {
148→                        mutated = true
149→                        /**
150→                         * Rebuild the suppression locator after each change in the AST as the offsets of the
151→                         * suppression hints might have changed.
152→                         */
153→                        ruleExecutionContext.rebuildSuppressionLocator()
154→                    }
155→                    val (line, col) = ruleExecutionContext.positionInTextLocator(offset)
156→                    errors.add(
157→                        Pair(
158→                            LintError(line, col, rule.ruleId, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected),
159→                            // It is assumed that a rule that emits that an error can be autocorrected, also
160→                            // does correct the error.
161→                            canBeAutoCorrected,
162→                        ),
163→                    )
164→                    // In trace mode report the violation immediately. The order in which violations are actually found might be different
165→                    // from the order in which they are reported. For debugging purposes it cn be helpful to know the exact order in which
166→                    // violations are being solved.
167→                    LOGGER.trace {
168→                        "Format violation: ${code.fileNameOrStdin()}:$line:$col: $errorMessage (${rule.ruleId})" +
169→                            canBeAutoCorrected.ifFalse { " [cannot be autocorrected]" }
170→                    }
171→                }
172→            }
173→        if (tripped) {
174→            visitorProvider
175→                .visitor()
176→                .invoke { rule ->
177→                    ruleExecutionContext.executeRule(
178→                        rule,
179→                        false,
180→                    ) { offset, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected ->
181→                        val (line, col) = ruleExecutionContext.positionInTextLocator(offset)
182→                        errors.add(
183→                            Pair(
184→                                LintError(line, col, rule.ruleId, errorMessage, canBeAutoCorrected),
185→                                // It is assumed that a rule only corrects an error after it has emitted an
186→                                // error and indicating that it actually can be autocorrected.
187→                                false,
188→                            ),
189→                        )
190→                    }
191→                }
192→        }
193→
194→        errors
195→            .sortedWith { (l), (r) -> if (l.line != r.line) l.line - r.line else l.col - r.col }
196→            .forEach { (e, corrected) -> callback(e, corrected) }
197→
198→        if (!mutated) {
199→            return code.content
200→        }
201→
202→        val formattedCode =
203→            ruleExecutionContext
204→                .rootNode
205→                .text
206→                .replace("\n", ruleExecutionContext.determineLineSeparator(code.content))
207→        return if (hasUTF8BOM) {
208→            UTF8_BOM + formattedCode
209→        } else {
210→            formattedCode
211→        }.also {
212→            LOGGER.debug("Finished with formatting file '${code.fileNameOrStdin()}'")
213→        }
214→    }
215→
216→    private fun RuleExecutionContext.determineLineSeparator(fileContent: String): String {
217→        val eol = editorConfig[END_OF_LINE_PROPERTY]
218→        return when {
219→            eol == crlf || eol != lf && fileContent.lastIndexOf('\r') != -1 -> "\r\n"
220→            else -> "\n"
221→        }
222→    }
223→
224→    /**
225→     * Generates Kotlin `.editorconfig` file section content based on a path to a file or directory. Given that path, all '.editorconfig'
226→     * files on that path are taken into account to determine the values of properties which are already used.
227→     *
228→     * @return Kotlin section editorconfig content. For example:
229→     * ```properties
230→     * final-newline=true
231→     * indent-size=4
232→     * ```
233→     */
234→    public fun generateKotlinEditorConfigSection(filePath: Path): String {
235→        val codeStyle =
236→            editorConfigOverride
237→                .properties[CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY]
238→                ?.parsed
239→                ?.safeAs<CodeStyleValue>()
240→                ?: CODE_STYLE_PROPERTY.defaultValue
241→        val rules =
242→            ruleProviders
243→                .map { it.createNewRuleInstance() }
244→                .distinctBy { it.ruleId }
245→                .toSet()
246→        return EditorConfigGenerator(fileSystem, editorConfigLoaderEc4j)
247→            .generateEditorconfig(
248→                rules,
249→                codeStyle,
250→                filePath,
251→            )
252→    }
253→
254→    /**
255→     * Reduce memory usage by cleaning internal caches.
256→     */
257→    public fun trimMemory() {
258→        THREAD_SAFE_EDITOR_CONFIG_CACHE.clear()
259→    }
260→
261→    /**
262→     * Get the list of files which will be accessed by KtLint when linting or formatting the given file or directory.
263→     * The API consumer can use this list to observe changes in '.editorconfig' files. Whenever such a change is
264→     * observed, the API consumer should call [reloadEditorConfigFile].
265→     * To avoid unnecessary access to the file system, it is best to call this method only once for the root of the
266→     * project which is to be [lint] or [format].
267→     */
268→    public fun editorConfigFilePaths(path: Path): List<Path> = EditorConfigFinder(editorConfigLoaderEc4j).findEditorConfigs(path)
269→
270→    /**
271→     * Reloads an '.editorconfig' file given that it is currently loaded into the KtLint cache. This method is intended
272→     * to be called by the API consumer when it is aware of changes in the '.editorconfig' file that should be taken
273→     * into account with next calls to [lint] and/or [format]. See [editorConfigFilePaths] to get the list of
274→     * '.editorconfig' files which need to be observed.
275→     */
276→    public fun reloadEditorConfigFile(path: Path) {
277→        THREAD_SAFE_EDITOR_CONFIG_CACHE.reloadIfExists(
278→            Resource.Resources.ofPath(path, StandardCharsets.UTF_8),
279→        )
280→    }
281→
282→    public fun transformToAst(code: Code): FileASTNode = createRuleExecutionContext(this, code).rootNode
283→
284→    public companion object {
285→        internal const val UTF8_BOM = "\uFEFF"
286→
287→        public const val STDIN_FILE: String = "<stdin>"
288→    }
289→}
290→

1→package com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api
 2→
 3→import com.pinterest.ktlint.rule.engine.api.KtLintRuleEngine.Companion.STDIN_FILE
 4→import org.jetbrains.kotlin.konan.file.file
 5→import java.io.File
 6→import java.nio.file.Path
 7→import kotlin.io.path.pathString
 8→
 9→/**
10→ * A representation of a block of code. Use one of the factory methods [fromFile], [fromPath], [fromSnippet] or [fromStdin] to instantiate.
11→ */
12→public class Code private constructor(
13→    public val content: String,
14→    public val fileName: String?,
15→    public val filePath: Path?,
16→    public val script: Boolean,
17→    public val isStdIn: Boolean,
18→) {
19→    public fun fileNameOrStdin(): String =
20→        if (isStdIn) {
21→            STDIN_FILE
22→        } else {
23→            fileName.orEmpty()
24→        }
25→
26→    public companion object {
27→        /**
28→         * Create [Code] from a [file] containing valid Kotlin code or script. The '.editorconfig' files on the path to [file] are taken
29→         * into account.
30→         */
31→        public fun fromFile(file: File): Code =
32→            Code(
33→                content = file.readText(),
34→                fileName = file.name,
35→                filePath = file.toPath(),
36→                script = file.name.endsWith(".kts", ignoreCase = true),
37→                isStdIn = false,
38→            )
39→
40→        /**
41→         * Create [Code] from a [path] to a file containing valid Kotlin code or script. The '.editorconfig' files on the path to [file] are
42→         * taken into account. This method is intended to be used in unit tests. In order to work with the Ktlint test file system it needs
43→         * to make additional call to get the file system which makes it slower compared to [fromFile]. Prefer to use [fromFile].
44→         */
45→        public fun fromPath(path: Path): Code {
46→            // Resolve the file based on the file system of the original path given.
47→            val file =
48→                path
49→                    .fileSystem
50→                    .file(path.pathString)
51→            return Code(
52→                content = file.readStrings().joinToString(separator = "\n"),
53→                fileName = file.name,
54→                filePath = path,
55→                script = file.name.endsWith(".kts", ignoreCase = true),
56→                isStdIn = false,
57→            )
58→        }
59→
60→        /**
61→         * The [content] represent a valid piece of Kotlin code or Kotlin script. The '.editorconfig' files on the filesystem are ignored as
62→         * the snippet is not associated with a file path. Use [Code.fromFile] for scanning a file while at the same time respecting the
63→         * '.editorconfig' files on the path to the file.
64→         */
65→        public fun fromSnippet(
66→            content: String,
67→            script: Boolean = false,
68→        ): Code =
69→            Code(
70→                content = content,
71→                filePath = null,
72→                fileName = null,
73→                script = script,
74→                isStdIn = true,
75→            )
76→
77→        /**
78→         * Create [Code] by reading the snippet from 'stdin'. No '.editorconfig' are taken into account.  The '.editorconfig' files on the
79→         * filesystem are ignored as the snippet is not associated with a file path. Use [Code.fromFile] for scanning a file while at the
80→         * same time respecting the '.editorconfig' files on the path to the file.
81→         */
82→        public fun fromStdin(): Code = fromSnippet(String(System.`in`.readBytes()))
83→    }
84→}
85→

1→# Release testing
  2→
  3→Before releasing a new version of KtLint, the release candidate is tested on a selection of open source projects to improve the quality of the release, and the documentation is tested for dead links.
  4→
  5→## Setup infrastructure
  6→
  7→1. Create a directory in which the projects are grouped:
  8→   ```shell
  9→   mkdir sample-projects
 10→   cd sample-projects
 11→   ```
 12→2. In the `sample-projects` directory, create a shell command script `exec-in-each-project.sh` with content below. This script is used to run the same command in each of the sample projects:
 13→   ```shell
 14→   # Examples of usage
 15→   #
 16→   #   Outstanding changes per project
 17→   #     ./exec-in-each-project.sh "git status"
 18→   #
 19→   #   Rollback outstanding changes per project
 20→   #     ./exec-in-each-project.sh "git reset --hard"
 21→   #
 22→   #   Run ktlint standard rules
 23→   #     ktlint --version && ktlint "**/*.kt" -v --relative -F
 24→   #
 25→   #   Commit changes of standard rules:
 26→   #     ./exec-in-each-project.sh "git commit -m \"ktlint (0.43.2) -F\""
 27→
 28→   find . -maxdepth 1 -type d \( ! -name . \) -exec bash -c "cd '{}' && pwd && $@" \;
 29→   ```
 30→3. Clone the repositories of the sample projects (feel free to change this selection):
 31→   ```shell
 32→   git clone https://github.com/google/iosched.git
 33→   git clone https://github.com/android/uamp.git
 34→   git clone https://github.com/siempredelao/StaticAnalysisTest
 35→   git clone https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx.coroutines.git
 36→   git clone https://github.com/square/okhttp.git
 37→   git clone https://github.com/detekt/detekt.git
 38→   git clone https://github.com/ExpediaGroup/graphql-kotlin.git
 39→   git clone https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx.serialization.git
 40→   ```
 41→4. Create an alias or script to run the latest released version of ktlint (note that this script will print the version as reference to which version is used):
 42→   ```shell
 43→   alias ktlint-prev="ktlint-0.47.1 $@" # Replace with the latest release version
 44→   ```
 45→   Note that `~/git/ktlint` is the directory in which the ktlint project is checked out and that `~/git/ktlint/ktlint` refers to the `ktlint` CLI-module.
 46→5. Create an alias or script to run the latest development-version of ktlint (note that this script will print the version and the datetime of compilation as reference to which version is used):
 47→   ```shell
 48→   alias ktlint-dev="echo 'ktlint-dev version:';ls -aslh ~/git/ktlint/ktlint/build-cli/run/ktlint;echo ' ';~/git/ktlint/ktlint/build/run/ktlint $@"
 49→   ```
 50→   Note that `~/git/ktlint` is the directory in which the ktlint project is checked out and that `~/git/ktlint/ktlint` refers to the `ktlint` CLI-module.
 51→
 52→## Cleanup from previous testing
 53→
 54→1. Reset the repositories of the sample projects:
 55→   ```shell
 56→   ./exec-in-each-project.sh "git reset --hard origin"
 57→   ```
 58→2. Optionally, pull latest changes of the sample projects:
 59→   ```shell
 60→   ./exec-in-each-project.sh "git pull"
 61→   ```
 62→3. Remove baseline file
 63→   ```shell
 64→   rm baseline.xml
 65→   ```
 66→
 67→## Testing a new release
 68→
 69→Pre-requisites:
 70→* All changes have been merged to master.
 71→* Latest version of master has been pulled to the local machine.
 72→* The latest released version of ktlint matches with command `ktlint`:
 73→   ```shell
 74→   ktlint-prev --version
 75→   ```
 76→* The latest version of master has been build on the local machine. And it is verified that the datetime of `ktlint-dev` (more or less) matches with the datetime of this compilation.
 77→   ```shell
 78→   ktlint-dev --version
 79→   ```
 80→
 81→Formatting projects in which ktlint is not used may result in a huge amount of fixes. The main focus of this test is to see what the effects are when upgrading ktlint in a project already formatted with latest released ktlint version.
 82→
 83→1. Format the sample projects with the *latest released* ktlint version:
 84→   ```shell
 85→   ktlint-prev -F --relative # Do not call this command via the "./exec-in-each-project.sh" script.
 86→   ```
 87→   Note: Ignore all output as this is the old version!
 88→2. Commit changes:
 89→   ```shell
 90→   ./exec-in-each-project.sh "git add --all && git commit -m \"Format with ktlint (xx.yy.zz) -F\""
 91→   ```
 92→   Repeat step 1 and 2 until no files are changed anymore.
 93→3. Create a new baseline file with the *latest released* ktlint version to ignore all lint violations that could not be autocorrected using the latest ktlint version:
 94→   ```shell
 95→   rm baseline.xml
 96→   ktlint-prev --baseline=baseline.xml --relative # Do not call this command via the "./exec-in-each-project.sh" script as we want one combined baseline.xml file for all projects.
 97→   ```
 98→4. Check that besides the `baseline.xml` no files are changed (in step 1 and 2 all violations which could be autocorrected have already been committed). Remaining violations which could not be autocorrected are saved in the `baseline.xml` which is stored outside the project directories.
 99→   ```shell
100→   ./exec-in-each-project.sh "git status"
101→   ```
102→   The `baseline.xml` file should only contain errors which can not be autocorrected.
103→5. Define `.editorconfig` in the integration test directory with content below. Also follow up the instructions mentioned:
104→   ```editorconfig
105→   root = true
106→   [*.{kt,kts}]
107→   # The open source projects that are used for release testing of ktlint contain a few '.editorconfig' files which need to
108→   # be changed:
109→   #   1) Disable the "root = true" property so that each project ultimately falls back on this file. In this way offending
110→   #      rules can be easily enabled/disabled for all test projects
111→   #   2) Add specific rules to project below
112→   #        graphql
113→   #          ktlint_standard_import-ordering = disabled
114→   #          ktlint_standard_package-name = disabled
115→   ktlint_experimental = enabled
116→   ktlint_standard = enabled
117→   ktlint_standard_filename = disabled
118→   ktlint_standard_no-wildcard-imports = disabled
119→   ktlint_standard_function-naming = disabled
120→   ktlint_standard_property-naming = disabled
121→   ```
122→6. Commit changes:
123→   ```shell
124→   ./exec-in-each-project.sh "git add --all && git commit -m \"Update .editorconfig to fallback to integration test settings\""
125→   ```
126→7. Lint with *latest development* version:
127→   ```shell
128→   ktlint-dev --baseline=baseline.xml --relative # Do not call this command via the "./exec-in-each-project.sh" script as we want to use the one combined baseline.xml file for all projects.
129→   # or when lots are violations are reported
130→   ktlint-dev --baseline=baseline.xml --relative --reporter=plain-summary # Do not call this command via the "./exec-in-each-project.sh" script as we want to use the one combined baseline.xml file for all projects.
131→   ```
132→   Inspect the output roughly (detailed inspection is done when formatting):
133→   * Is the amount of logging messages comparable to before? If not, are the changes intended?
134→   * Are violations related to rules that have actually been added or changed?
135→8. Format with *latest development* version:
136→   ```shell
137→   ktlint-dev -F --baseline=baseline.xml --relative # Do not call this command via the "./exec-in-each-project.sh" script as we want to use the one combined baseline.xml file for all projects.
138→   ```
139→   Inspect the output carefully:
140→    * If you see an error like below, then this version obviously may *not* be released. It is best to fix this error before continuing with testing and validating!
141→      ```plain
142→      Internal Error (...) in file '...' at position '0:0. Please create a ticket at https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues ...
143→      ```
144→   * Ideally, no violations are shown. This means that all violations have been autocorrected.
145→   * Violations which could not be autocorrected should be validated for correctness but do not block the release as most likely this is intended behavior.
146→   * If a violation is shown which is not marked as being "can not be autocorrected" this means that during autocorrect of another violation a new violations has been introduced. This should be fixed before releasing especially when the next format introduces the original violation again which of course would result in an endless loop.
147→9. Inspect all fixed violations, Of course inspection similar violations tens of times does not make sense. At least check different types of violations a couple of times. Commit changes which do not need to be inspected again:
148→   ```shell
149→   ./exec-in-each-project.sh "git add --all && git commit -m \"Fixed with latest development version\""
150→   ```
151→10. Rerun lint with *latest development* version:
152→    ```shell
153→    ktlint-dev --baseline=baseline.xml --relative # Do not call this command via the "./exec-in-each-project.sh" script as we want to use the one combined baseline.xml file for all projects.
154→    ```
155→    No violations, except error that can not be autocorrected, should be expected.
156→
157→## Checking documentation
158→[The documentation for KtLint](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/) should be checked for dead links.
159→Follow the instructions for building the documentation in `/docs/readme.md`, and use a tool such as [Broken Link Checker Tool](https://www.deadlinkchecker.com/website-dead-link-checker.asp) to find broken links.
160→

conversation_history.name: read
conversation_history.content:
1→!!! important
  2→    Up and until Ktlint version `0.47`, experimental were located in a separate `experimental` rule set. As of Ktlint version `0.48`, each rule set can optionally define experimental rules.
  3→
  4→All experimental rules described below are part of the `standard` rule set of Ktlint. To enable all experimental rules (from all rule sets), set `editorconfig` property below:
  5→```ini
  6→[*.{kt,kts}]
  7→ktlint_experimental=enabled
  8→```
  9→Also see [enable/disable specific rules](../configuration-ktlint/#disabled-rules).
 10→
 11→## Binary expression wrapping
 12→
 13→Wraps binary expression at the operator reference whenever the binary expression does not fit on the line. In case the binary expression is nested, the expression is evaluated from outside to inside. If the left and right hand sides of the binary expression, after wrapping, fit on a single line then the inner binary expressions will not be wrapped. If one or both inner binary expression still do not fit on a single after wrapping of the outer binary expression, then each of those inner binary expressions will be wrapped.
 14→
 15→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 16→
 17→    ```kotlin
 18→    fun foo() {
 19→        // Assume that the last allowed character is
 20→        // at the X character on the right                       X
 21→        if ((leftHandSideExpression && rightHandSideExpression) ||
 22→            (leftHandSideLongExpression &&
 23→                rightHandSideLongExpression)) {
 24→            // do something
 25→        }
 26→    }
 27→    ```
 28→
 29→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 30→
 31→    ```kotlin
 32→    fun foo() {
 33→        // Assume that the last allowed character is
 34→        // at the X character on the right                       X
 35→        if ((leftHandSideExpression && rightHandSideExpression) || (leftHandSideLongExpression && rightHandSideLongExpression)) {
 36→            // do something
 37→        }
 38→    }
 39→    ```
 40→
 41→Rule id: `binary-expression-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
 42→
 43→## Blank line before declarations
 44→
 45→Requires a blank line before any class or function declaration. No blank line is required between the class signature and the first declaration in the class. In a similar way, a blank line is required before any list of top level or class properties. No blank line is required before local properties or between consecutive properties.
 46→
 47→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 48→
 49→    ```kotlin
 50→    const val foo1 = "foo1"
 51→
 52→    class FooBar {
 53→        val foo2 = "foo2"
 54→        val foo3 = "foo3"
 55→
 56→        fun bar1() {
 57→           val foo4 = "foo4"
 58→           val foo5 = "foo5"
 59→        }
 60→
 61→        fun bar2() = "bar"
 62→
 63→        val foo6 = "foo3"
 64→        val foo7 = "foo4"
 65→
 66→        enum class Foo {}
 67→    }
 68→    ```
 69→
 70→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 71→
 72→    ```kotlin
 73→    const val foo1 = "foo1"
 74→    class FooBar {
 75→        val foo2 = "foo2"
 76→        val foo3 = "foo3"
 77→        fun bar1() {
 78→           val foo4 = "foo4"
 79→           val foo5 = "foo5"
 80→        }
 81→        fun bar2() = "bar"
 82→        val foo6 = "foo3"
 83→        val foo7 = "foo4"
 84→        enum class Foo {}
 85→    }
 86→    ```
 87→
 88→Rule id: `blank-line-before-declaration` (`standard` rule set)
 89→
 90→!!! Note
 91→This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.
 92→
 93→## Discouraged comment location
 94→
 95→Detect discouraged comment locations (no autocorrect).
 96→
 97→!!! note
 98→    Kotlin allows comments to be placed almost everywhere. As this can lead to code which is hard to read, most of them will never be used in practice. Ideally each rule takes comments at all possible locations into account. Sometimes this is really hard and not worth the effort. By explicitly forbidding such comment locations, the development of those rules becomes a bit easier. 
 99→
100→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
101→
102→    ```kotlin
103→    fun <T> /* some comment */ foo(t: T) = "some-result"
104→
105→    fun foo() {
106→        if (true)
107→            // some comment
108→            bar()
109→    }
110→    ```
111→
112→Rule id: `discouraged-comment-location` (`standard` rule set)
113→
114→## Disallow empty lines at start of class body
115→
116→Detect blank lines at start of a class body.
117→
118→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
119→
120→    ```kotlin
121→    class Foo {
122→        val foo = "foo"
123→    }
124→    ```
125→
126→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
127→
128→    ```kotlin
129→    class Foo {
130→
131→        val foo = "foo"
132→    }
133→    ```
134→
135→Rule id: `no-empty-first-line-in-class-body` (`standard` rule set)
136→
137→!!! Note
138→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.
139→
140→## Disallow consecutive comments
141→
142→Consecutive comments are disallowed in following cases:
143→- Any mix of a consecutive kdoc, a block comment or an EOL comment unless separated by a blank line in between
144→- Consecutive KDocs (even when separated by a blank line)
145→- Consecutive block comments (even when separated by a blank line)
146→
147→Consecutive EOL comments are always allowed as they are often used instead of a block comment.
148→
149→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
150→
151→    ```kotlin
152→    // An EOL comment
153→    // may be followed by another EOL comment
154→    val foo = "foo"
155→
156→    // Different comment types (including KDoc) may be consecutive ..
157→
158→    /*
159→     * ... but do need to be separated by a blank line ...
160→     */
161→
162→    /**
163→      * ... but a KDoc can not be followed by an EOL or a block comment or another KDoc
164→      */
165→    fun bar() = "bar"
166→
167→    ```
168→
169→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
170→
171→    ```kotlin
172→    /*
173→     * Block comments can not be consecutive ...
174→     */
175→    /*
176→     * ... even not when separated by a new line.
177→     */
178→    val bar = "bar" 
179→
180→    /**
181→      * A KDoc can not be followed by a block comment or an EOL comment or another KDOC
182→      */
183→
184→    // ... even not when separated by a new line.
185→    ```
186→
187→Rule id: `no-consecutive-comments` (`standard` rule set)
188→
189→!!! Note
190→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.
191→
192→## Function signature
193→
194→Rewrites the function signature to a single line when possible (e.g. when not exceeding the `max_line_length` property) or a multiline signature otherwise. In case of function with a body expression, the body expression is placed on the same line as the function signature when not exceeding the `max_line_length` property. Optionally the function signature can be forced to be written as a multiline signature in case the function has more than a specified number of parameters (`.editorconfig` property `ktlint_function_signature_wrapping_rule_always_with_minimum_parameters`)
195→
196→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
197→
198→    ```kotlin
199→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
200→    // at the X character on the right           X
201→    fun foooooooo(
202→        a: Any,
203→        b: Any,
204→        c: Any
205→    ): String {
206→        // body
207→    }
208→
209→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
210→    // at the X character on the right           X
211→    fun bar(a: Any, b: Any, c: Any): String {
212→        // body
213→    }
214→
215→    // When wrapping of body is set to 'default'.
216→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
217→    // at the X character on the right           X
218→    fun f(a: Any, b: Any): String = "some-result"
219→        .uppercase()
220→
221→    // When wrapping of body is set to 'multiline'
222→    // or 'always'.
223→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
224→    // at the X character on the right           X
225→    fun f(a: Any, b: Any): String =
226→        "some-result"
227→            .uppercase()
228→    ```
229→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
230→
231→    ```kotlin
232→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
233→    // at the X character on the right           X
234→    fun foooooooo(a: Any, b: Any, c: Any): String {
235→        // body
236→    }
237→
238→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
239→    // at the X character on the right           X
240→    fun bar(
241→        a: Any,
242→        b: Any,
243→        c: Any
244→    ): String {
245→        // body
246→    }
247→
248→    // When wrapping of body is set to 'default'.
249→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
250→    // at the X character on the right           X
251→    fun f(a: Any, b: Any): String =
252→        "some-result"
253→            .uppercase()
254→
255→    // When wrapping of body is set to 'multiline'
256→    // or 'always'.
257→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
258→    // at the X character on the right           X
259→    fun f(a: Any, b: Any): String = "some-result"
260→        .uppercase()
261→    ```
262→
263→Rule id: `function-signature` (`standard` rule set)
264→
265→## If else bracing
266→
267→If at least one branch of an if-else statement or an if-else-if statement is wrapped between curly braces then all branches should be wrapped between braces.
268→
269→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
270→
271→    ```kotlin
272→    fun foo(value: int) {
273→        if (value > 0) {
274→            doSomething()
275→        } else if (value < 0) {
276→            doSomethingElse()
277→        } else {
278→            doSomethingElse2()
279→        }
280→    }
281→    ```
282→
283→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
284→
285→    ```kotlin
286→    fun foo(value: int) {
287→        if (value > 0)
288→            doSomething()
289→        else if (value < 0) {
290→            doSomethingElse()
291→        } else
292→            doSomethingElse2()
293→    }
294→    ```
295→
296→Rule id: `if-else-bracing` (`standard` rule set)
297→
298→!!! Note
299→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.
300→
301→## If else wrapping
302→
303→A single line if-statement should be kept simple. It may contain no more than one else-branch. The branches may not be wrapped in a block.
304→
305→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
306→
307→    ```kotlin
308→    fun foobar() {
309→        if (true) foo()
310→        if (true) foo() else bar()
311→    }
312→    ```
313→
314→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
315→
316→    ```kotlin
317→    fun foobar() {
318→        if (true) if (false) foo() else bar()
319→        if (true) bar() else if (false) foo() else bar()
320→        if (true) { foo() } else bar()
321→        if (true) bar() else { if (false) foo() else bar() }
322→    }
323→    ```
324→
325→Rule id: `if-else-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
326→
327→!!! Note
328→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.
329→
330→## Naming
331→
332→### Function naming
333→
334→Enforce naming of function. 
335→
336→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
337→
338→    ```kotlin
339→    fun foo() {}
340→    fun fooBar() {}
341→    ```
342→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint Test"
343→
344→    ```kotlin
345→    @Test
346→    fun `Some name`() {}
347→
348→    @Test
349→    fun do_something() {}
350→    ```
351→
352→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
353→
354→    ```kotlin
355→    fun Foo() {}
356→    fun Foo_Bar() {}
357→    fun `Some name`() {}
358→    fun do_something() {}
359→    ```
360→
361→!!! note
362→    Functions in files which import a class from package `org.junit`, `org.testng` or `kotlin.test` are considered to be test functions. Functions in such classes are allowed to have underscores in the name. Or function names can be specified between backticks and do not need to adhere to the normal naming convention.
363→
364→This rule can also be suppressed with the IntelliJ IDEA inspection suppression `FunctionName`.
365→
366→Rule id: `function-naming` (`standard` rule set)
367→
368→### Package naming
369→
370→Enforce naming of package.
371→
372→This rule can also be suppressed with the IntelliJ IDEA inspection suppression `PackageName`.
373→
374→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
375→
376→    ```kotlin
377→    package foo
378→    package foo.foo
379→    package foo_bar
380→    package foo.foo_bar
381→    ```
382→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
383→
384→    ```kotlin
385→    package Foo
386→    package foo.Foo
387→    package `foo bar`
388→    package foo.`foo bar`
389→    ```
390→
391→Rule id: `package-naming` (`standard` rule set)
392→
393→### Property naming
394→
395→Enforce naming of property.
396→
397→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
398→
399→    ```kotlin
400→    val FOO_1 = Foo()
401→    val FOO_BAR_1 = "FOO-BAR"
402→
403→    var foo1: Foo = Foo()
404→
405→    class Bar {
406→        const val FOO_2 = "foo"
407→        const val FOO_BAR_2 = "FOO-BAR"
408→
409→        val foo2 = "foo"
410→        val fooBar2 = "foo-bar"
411→
412→        // Backing property
413→        private val _elementList = mutableListOf<Element>()
414→        val elementList: List<Element>
415→            get() = _elementList
416→    }
417→    ```
418→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
419→
420→    ```kotlin
421→    val Foo1 = Foo()
422→    val FooBar1 = "FOO-BAR"
423→
424→    var FOO_1: Foo = Foo()
425→
426→    class Bar {
427→        const val foo2 = "foo"
428→        const val fooBar2 = "FOO-BAR"
429→
430→        val FOO2 = "foo"
431→        val FOO_BAR_2 = "foo-bar"
432→
433→        // Incomplete backing property as public property 'elementList1' is missing
434→        private val _elementList1 = mutableListOf<Element>()
435→
436→        // Invalid backing property as '_elementList2' is not a private property
437→        val _elementList2 = mutableListOf<Element>()
438→        val elementList2: List<Element>
439→            get() = _elementList2
440→    }
441→    ```
442→
443→!!! note
444→    Top level `val` properties and `const val` properties have to be written in screaming snake notation. Local `val` and `const val` are written in lower camel case.
445→
446→This rule can also be suppressed with the IntelliJ IDEA inspection suppression `PropertyName`.
447→
448→Rule id: `property-naming` (`standard` rule set)
449→
450→## No empty file
451→
452→A kotlin (script) file should not be empty. It needs to contain at least one declaration. Files only contain a package and/or import statements are as of that disallowed.
453→
454→Rule id: `no-empty-file`
455→
456→## No single line block comments
457→
458→A single line block comment should be replaced with an EOL comment when possible.
459→
460→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
461→
462→    ```kotlin
463→    /*
464→     * Some comment
465→     */
466→    val foo = "foo" // Some comment
467→    val foo = { /* no-op */ }
468→
469→    /* ktlint-disable foo-rule-id bar-rule-id */
470→    val foo = "foo"
471→    /* ktlint-enable foo-rule-id bar-rule-id */
472→    ```
473→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
474→
475→    ```kotlin
476→    /* Some comment */
477→    val foo = "foo" /* Some comment */
478→    ```
479→
480→Rule id: `no-single-line-block-comment` (`standard` rule set)
481→
482→## Spacing
483→
484→### No blank lines in list
485→
486→Disallow blank lines to be used in lists before the first element, between elements, and after the last element.
487→
488→*Super type*
489→
490→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
491→
492→    ```kotlin
493→    class FooBar:
494→        Foo,
495→        Bar {
496→        // body
497→    }
498→    ```
499→
500→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
501→
502→    ```kotlin
503→    class FooBar:
504→
505→        Foo,
506→
507→        Bar
508→
509→    {
510→        // body
511→    }
512→    ```
513→
514→*Type argument list*
515→
516→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
517→
518→    ```kotlin
519→    val foobar: FooBar<
520→        Foo,
521→        Bar,
522→        > = FooBar(Foo(), Bar())
523→    ```
524→
525→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
526→
527→    ```kotlin
528→    val foobar: FooBar<
529→
530→        Foo,
531→
532→        Bar,
533→
534→        > = FooBar(Foo(), Bar())
535→    ```
536→
537→*Type constraint list*
538→

783→## Wrapping
784→
785→### Content receiver wrapping
786→
787→Wraps the content receiver list to a separate line regardless of maximum line length. If the maximum line length is configured and is exceeded, wrap the context receivers and if needed its projection types to separate lines.
788→
789→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
790→
791→    ```kotlin
792→    // ALways wrap regardless of whether max line length is set
793→    context(Foo)
794→    fun fooBar()
795→
796→    // Wrap each context receiver to a separate line when the
797→    // entire context receiver list does not fit on a single line
798→    context(
799→        Fooooooooooooooooooo1,
800→        Foooooooooooooooooooooooooooooo2
801→    )
802→    fun fooBar()
803→
804→    // Wrap each context receiver to a separate line when the
805→    // entire context receiver list does not fit on a single line.
806→    // Also, wrap each of it projection types in case a context
807→    // receiver does not fit on a single line after it has been
808→    // wrapped.
809→    context(

812→            Bar

825→    // fit on a single line
826→    context(Fooooooooooooooooooo1, Foooooooooooooooooooooooooooooo2)
827→    fun fooBar()
828→
829→    // Should be wrapped when the entire context receiver list does not
830→    // fit on a single line. Also, it should wrap each of it projection
831→    // type in case a context receiver does not fit on a single line 
832→    // after it has been wrapped.
833→    context(Foooooooooooooooo<Foo, Bar>)
834→    fun fooBar()
835→    ```

837→Rule id: `context-receiver-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)

839→### Enum wrapping
840→
841→An enum should be a single line, or each enum entry has to be placed on a separate line. In case the enumeration contains enum entries and declarations those are to be separated by a blank line.

content.thinking:
**Evaluating rerun conditions**

I need to figure out when to rerun the formatting. The criteria should be whether the first autocorrect pass found any correctable violations or if, during a second lint-only pass, we detect anything that can be auto-corrected. It turns out that rerun formatting applies when "format finds any lint violations... that can be auto-corrected." So, if the second pass finds any such issues, we should rerun the formatting. This plan seems better and more accurate!**Clarifying format errors**

I need to consider how the current format collects errors from both passes. In the second pass, errors are always marked as "corrected=false," even if they can be auto-corrected. This creates a situation where the callback might indicate that an error can be auto-corrected but hasn’t been corrected. If the second pass finds any errors that can be auto-corrected, it means the formatting didn’t address them. So, rerunning should only happen when there are still auto-correctable errors after formatting!**Examining rule execution order**

I’m thinking about the rule execution order and how multiple trips are needed to resolve all errors. After the first trip, if there are still auto-correctable errors, we definitely need a rerun. If the first pass fixes something but there are still no existing errors, a rerun might not be necessary. However, the requirement says to rerun whenever any auto-correctable violations were found, even if corrected during the same run. This can get tricky when one rule introduces an issue that another rule could address!**Understanding correction logic**

In the first pass, rule B checks for issues but if rule A later modifies the code, that can create problems for rule B. If the second pass identifies that B has an auto-correctable issue, a rerun would happen because A initially introduced violations. But if A modifies something without marking it as auto-correctable, that's a concern too. The spec suggests rerunning whenever any auto-correctable violations are found, even if it seems clean, which can lead to unnecessary reruns. It feels a bit tangled!**Clarifying rerun logic**

I’m dissecting how the formatting process should work. After the first pass, if there are no remaining auto-correctable errors, we should stop. We need to check if there are any violations that can be fixed and whether corrections were made. The process suggests we should rerun if we find correctable errors in a lint-only pass. If there are remaining ones, we should rerun; but if no errors are found, we can stop. The requirement is tricky—especially regarding which errors are reported after each run, as we want to provide clarity on corrections made!**Defining rerun conditions**

I'm looking into the rerun logic for formatting. We should repeat the format until there are no remaining auto-correctable errors. The text states that if any violations are found, a rerun is necessary. There might be up to three reruns needed after corrections, especially when complex dependencies are involved. The simplest approach is to rerun if anything was mutated, ensuring stable outputs. However, we need to handle cases where errors are found but no changes were made. So, I plan to implement a loop that keeps track of auto-correctable violations and rerun as needed.**Clarifying code behavior**

I need to clarify some details about how the current version handles file processing. If `mutated` is false, the process should return the original content even if there are non-autocorrectable errors. This means we'll report those errors but stop the loop when no auto-correctable violations are found. 

I need to ensure that when rerunning, we respect the UTF-8 BOM handling, as it's important for maintaining file integrity. It's crucial to avoid adding a BOM multiple times. I'll also look into creating tests to reinforce this behavior, ensuring our formatting works as expected.

471→/**
472→ * A dummy rule for testing. Optionally the rule can be created with a lambda to be executed for each node visited.
473→ */
474→private open class DummyRule(
475→    val block: (node: ASTNode) -> Unit = {},
476→) : Rule(
477→    ruleId = RuleId("test:dummy"),
478→    about = About(),
479→) {
480→    override fun beforeVisitChildNodes(
481→        node: ASTNode,
482→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
483→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
484→    ) {
485→        block(node)
486→    }
487→}
488→
489→/**
490→ * A dummy rule for testing
491→ */
492→private class AutoCorrectErrorRule : Rule(
493→    ruleId = AUTOCORRECT_ERROR_RULE_ID,
494→    about = About(),
495→) {
496→    override fun beforeVisitChildNodes(
497→        node: ASTNode,
498→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
499→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
500→    ) {
501→        if (node.elementType == REGULAR_STRING_PART) {
502→            when (node.text) {
503→                STRING_VALUE_TO_BE_AUTOCORRECTED -> {
504→                    emit(node.startOffset, ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_BE_AUTOCORRECTED, true)
505→                    if (autoCorrect) {
506→                        (node as LeafElement).rawReplaceWithText(STRING_VALUE_AFTER_AUTOCORRECT)
507→                    }
508→                }
509→                STRING_VALUE_NOT_TO_BE_CORRECTED ->
510→                    emit(node.startOffset, ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_NOT_BE_AUTOCORRECTED, false)
511→            }
512→        }
513→    }
514→
515→    companion object {
516→        val AUTOCORRECT_ERROR_RULE_ID = RuleId("test:auto-correct")
517→        const val STRING_VALUE_TO_BE_AUTOCORRECTED = "string-value-to-be-autocorrected"
518→        const val STRING_VALUE_NOT_TO_BE_CORRECTED = "string-value-not-to-be-corrected"
519→        const val STRING_VALUE_AFTER_AUTOCORRECT = "string-value-after-autocorrect"
520→        const val ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_BE_AUTOCORRECTED = "This string value is not allowed and can be autocorrected"
521→        const val ERROR_MESSAGE_CAN_NOT_BE_AUTOCORRECTED =
522→            "This string value is not allowed but can not be autocorrected"
523→    }
524→}
525→
526→/**
527→ * Rule in style starting from ktlint 0.47.x in which a rule can can override method [Rule.beforeFirstNode],
528→ * [Rule.beforeVisitChildNodes], [Rule.afterVisitChildNodes] and [Rule.afterLastNode]. For each invocation to
529→ * this method a [RuleExecutionCall] is added to the list of previously calls made.
530→ */
531→private class SimpleTestRule(
532→    private val ruleExecutionCalls: MutableList<RuleExecutionCall>,
533→    ruleId: RuleId,
534→    visitorModifiers: Set<VisitorModifier> = emptySet(),
535→    private val stopTraversalInBeforeFirstNode: Boolean = false,
536→    private val stopTraversalInBeforeVisitChildNodes: (ASTNode) -> Boolean = { false },
537→    private val stopTraversalInAfterVisitChildNodes: (ASTNode) -> Boolean = { false },
538→    private val stopTraversalInAfterLastNode: Boolean = false,
539→) : Rule(
540→    ruleId = ruleId,
541→    about = About(),
542→    visitorModifiers,
543→) {
544→    override fun beforeFirstNode(editorConfig: EditorConfig) {
545→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(RuleExecutionCall(ruleId, BEFORE_FIRST))
546→        if (stopTraversalInBeforeFirstNode) {
547→            stopTraversalOfAST()
548→        }
549→    }
550→
551→    override fun beforeVisitChildNodes(
552→        node: ASTNode,
553→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
554→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
555→    ) {
556→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(node.toRuleExecutionCall(ruleId, BEFORE_CHILDREN))
557→        if (stopTraversalInBeforeVisitChildNodes(node)) {
558→            stopTraversalOfAST()
559→        }
560→    }
561→
562→    override fun afterVisitChildNodes(
563→        node: ASTNode,
564→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
565→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
566→    ) {
567→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(node.toRuleExecutionCall(ruleId, AFTER_CHILDREN))
568→        if (stopTraversalInAfterVisitChildNodes(node)) {
569→            stopTraversalOfAST()
570→        }
571→    }
572→
573→    override fun afterLastNode() {
574→        ruleExecutionCalls.add(RuleExecutionCall(ruleId, AFTER_LAST))
575→        if (stopTraversalInAfterLastNode) {
576→            stopTraversalOfAST()
577→        }
578→    }
579→
580→    private fun ASTNode.toRuleExecutionCall(
581→        ruleId: RuleId,
582→        ruleMethod: RuleExecutionCall.RuleMethod,
583→    ) = RuleExecutionCall(
584→        ruleId,
585→        ruleMethod,
586→        visitNodeType,
587→        elementType,
588→        if (elementType == CLASS) {
589→            findChildByType(IDENTIFIER)?.text
590→        } else {
591→            null
592→        },
593→    )
594→
595→    companion object {
596→        val RULE_ID_A = RuleId("simple-test:a")
597→        val RULE_ID_B = RuleId("simple-test:b")
598→        val RULE_ID_C = RuleId("simple-test:c")
599→        val RULE_ID_D = RuleId("simple-test:d")
600→        val RULE_ID_STOP_TRAVERSAL = RuleId("simple-test:stop-traversal")
601→    }
602→}
603→
604→private data class RuleExecutionCall(
605→    val ruleId: RuleId,
606→    val ruleMethod: RuleMethod,
607→    val visitNodeType: VisitNodeType? = null,
608→    val elementType: IElementType? = null,
609→    val classIdentifier: String? = null,
610→) {
611→    enum class RuleMethod { BEFORE_FIRST, BEFORE_CHILDREN, VISIT, AFTER_CHILDREN, AFTER_LAST }
612→
613→    enum class VisitNodeType { ROOT, CHILD }
614→}
615→
616→private val ASTNode.visitNodeType: RuleExecutionCall.VisitNodeType
617→    get() =
618→        if (isRoot()) {
619→            ROOT
620→        } else {
621→            CHILD
622→        }
623→
624→private fun getResourceAsText(path: String) =
625→    (ClassLoader.getSystemClassLoader().getResourceAsStream(path) ?: throw RuntimeException("$path not found"))
626→        .bufferedReader()
627→        .readText()
628→
629→private data class CallbackResult(
630→    val line: Int,
631→    val col: Int,
632→    val ruleId: RuleId,
633→    val detail: String,
634→    val canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean,
635→    val corrected: Boolean,
636→)
637→
638→/**
639→ * This rule throws an exception when it is visited more than once.
640→ */
641→private class WithStateRule : Rule(
642→    ruleId = RuleId("test:with-state"),
643→    about = About(),
644→) {
645→    private var hasNotBeenVisitedYet = true
646→
647→    override fun beforeFirstNode(editorConfig: EditorConfig) {
648→        check(hasNotBeenVisitedYet) {
649→            "Rule has been visited before"
650→        }
651→        hasNotBeenVisitedYet = false
652→    }
653→
654→    override fun beforeVisitChildNodes(
655→        node: ASTNode,
656→        autoCorrect: Boolean,
657→        emit: (offset: Int, errorMessage: String, canBeAutoCorrected: Boolean) -> Unit,
658→    ) {
659→        emit(node.startOffset, "Fake violation which can be autocorrected", true)
660→    }
661→}
